items.title: Anthropic Mythos: $1M+ in tokens per week, 5x faster vulnerability detection — cybersecurity becomes AI's next pricing frontier
items.idea: Palo Alto Networks burned through $1M+ in tokens testing Anthropic's Mythos and found 2+ dozen critical vulnerabilities in ~3 weeks — 5x faster than existing tools. Mythos is priced at 6x Opus per token. Anthropic has not broadly released Mythos due to dual-use fears. A top VC incubator head says his personal AI bill is already crossing seven figures per month. The FBI recorded $21B in US cyberattack losses last year (up from $16.6B).
items.angle: Clearest proof yet that AI pricing power has no ceiling in domains where the alternative cost (a breach) vastly exceeds compute cost. Anthropic IPO filed while Mythos is in controlled release — sequencing is not accidental.
items.tweetA: Palo Alto tested Anthropic's Mythos for 3 weeks and burned through $1M+ in tokens. Found 2+ dozen critical vulnerabilities — 5x faster than anything else. Mythos is 6x more expensive per token than Opus. And companies are saying: worth it. This is what pricing power actually looks like.
items.tweetB: FBI: $21B in US cyberattack losses last year. Anthropic Mythos: burns $1M+ in tokens per company per engagement. The math is simple — if one breach costs $50M and Mythos prevention costs $2M, CISO budgets are going vertical. The velvet-rope AI economy is real, and cybersecurity is the second door after coding.

items.title: Two classes of AI: $20/month consumer product vs $1M+/month enterprise weapon — the K-curve is structural and accelerating
items.idea: The AI economy is bifurcating into a K-curve: consumers get commoditized Gemini/ChatGPT, while top developers and cybersecurity executives pay price-almost-no-object for real compute. Key cost architecture: Palo Alto uses Mythos to plan attacks, then directs cheaper Opus 4.7 to execute — orchestration at the frontier, execution at the commodity layer. OpenAI's GPT-5.5-cyber is competing but Mythos is 'somewhat more advanced' per UK AI testing agency.
items.angle: The orchestration/execution split is the AI infrastructure investment framework for the next cycle. Who owns the planning layer? That is where pricing power concentrates.
items.tweetA: Most people are using AI wrong. The $20/month tier is not the product — it's the funnel. The real AI is $1M+/month for cybersecurity, trading, and legal. Anthropic Mythos. GPT-5.5-cyber. The K-curve is here. Two classes of capability, two classes of outcome.
items.tweetB: Palo Alto's Mythos cost hack: use the expensive model to PLAN the attack, use cheap Opus 4.7 to EXECUTE it. This is the AI cost architecture for the next decade — orchestration at the frontier, execution at the commodity layer. The question is: who owns the orchestration layer?

items.source: Bloomberg / John Authers — Points of Return
items.title: Tech's 33% 3-month outperformance is the best on record this century — the previous best was post-9/11
items.idea: The IT sector's rolling 3-month outperformance of the S&P ex-tech just hit 33% — the best reading on record since the dot-com bubble. Previous best was post-9/11. US S&P ex-tech is BELOW its level from 3 months ago and has barely made up any ground on EAFE.
items.angle: All US outperformance vs the world is 100% technology. Strip tech out and America is losing. Owning US equities broad is not a geopolitical hedge — owning US tech specifically is.
items.tweetA: Here is the S&P fact nobody is saying plainly: US equities ex-tech are BELOW their 3-month-ago levels and have barely outperformed Europe. Every bit of American equity alpha is inside the IT sector. 33% outperformance — best reading since the dot-com collapse. You are not owning a country. You are owning a monopoly.
items.tweetB: The previous record for tech's 3-month outperformance was post-9/11 — when tech was immune to geopolitical damage. Same logic applies to Hormuz now. Geography stopped mattering for capital allocation the day TSMC started shipping wafers. The Authers chart is genuinely historic.

charts.desc: Rolling 3-month tech outperformance vs S&P ex-tech showing 33% as best on record. US vs global IT since 2024 election. S&P ex-tech vs EAFE — essentially flat.

items.source: Bloomberg / John Authers — Points of Return
items.title: Hormuz effectively closed for 3 months; S&P on 9-day win streak — geography no longer constrains capital allocation
items.idea: Bloomberg MAP SHIP confirms Hormuz is effectively closed for 3 months. Yet the S&P 500 posted its ninth consecutive day of gains, crossing 7,600 for the first time. Authers frames this as technology freeing capital from geography.
items.angle: Pair trade: long global semiconductors, short energy-importing equities without tech shields. The geography-vs-technology framing is the macro narrative of 2026.
items.tweetA: The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed for 3 months. The S&P just printed 9 consecutive up days. That is not a contradiction — that is the market telling you geography is obsolete for capital. The world's oil chokepoint is frozen. The world's AI chokepoint is melting. Follow the compute.
items.tweetB: Bloomberg MAP SHIP confirms Hormuz basically closed for 3 months. Energy-importing nations are bleeding. The S&P is at record highs. The divergence is not irrational — it is the most rational pricing of relative technological advantage in market history. Geography is a tax on energy. It is a subsidy on compute.

charts.desc: Bloomberg terminal chart of Hormuz story count spiking and holding elevated. War dashboard showing nightmare scenario realized.

items.source: Bloomberg / John Authers — Points of Return
items.title: Micron topped $1.2T market cap — grew the equivalent of McDonald's entire market value in ONE week
items.idea: One week ago Micron crossed $1 trillion. Now it is above $1.2 trillion. The growth over 7 days equals the entire market cap of McDonald's or Verizon. HBM is sold out through 2026. Samsung 45K-worker strike adds further supply pressure.
items.angle: Micron at $1.2T with HBM sold out through 2026 means the DRAM supply story is in execution, not thesis. This is the physical bottleneck of the AI buildout.
items.tweetA: Micron crossed $1T market cap last week. This week it is at $1.2T. The growth over 7 days equals the entire valuation of McDonald's. Hormuz is closed, semiconductor materials are threatened, HBM is sold out through 2026. $MU is not a stock. It is a physical bottleneck.
items.tweetB: The Hormuz closure that is killing energy stocks is BULLISH for semis. Restricted supply of materials plus record demand plus HBM sold out 2026 equals Micron adding a McDonald's of market cap in one week. Geography is a tax on energy. It is a subsidy on compute.

items.source: Bloomberg / John Authers — Points of Return
items.title: JOLTS surges to highest pre-pandemic level — AI jobpocalypse absent from data 4 years into the generative AI era
items.idea: April JOLTS shows openings at highest pre-pandemic level; more than one vacancy per unemployed worker. AI-driven disruption caused ~55,000 planned layoffs in 2025 — 5% of total cuts, ranked 5th. Bloomberg Economics: 27% of workers in advanced economies (~120M) likely meaningfully affected over time. Construction worker demand up 430% since ChatGPT launch.
items.angle: 27% of 120 million workers is a slow-motion event, not absence of event. The 430% surge in data center construction jobs is a leading indicator that ends when the buildout phase slows.
items.tweetA: We are 4 years into the generative AI era and JOLTS just printed the highest job openings since before the pandemic. AI displaced 55K jobs in 2025 — ranked 5th among layoff reasons. The jobpocalypse is taking its time. But 27% of 120 million workers is not 'no risk.' It is a slow fuse.
items.tweetB: Data center construction jobs up 430% since ChatGPT launched. That is the AI economy you can see right now. The 27% of workers who get displaced by AI? That is the economy you cannot see yet. Both are real. The lag between technological shock and employment impact has always been the hardest thing to time.

charts.desc: JOLTS vacancies vs unemployed workers. AI-exposed job categories by share of workforce.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Gold overtook US Treasuries as world's largest reserve asset — the ECB confirmed what reserve managers already knew
items.idea: ECB report formally acknowledges gold represents a larger share of official reserves than US Treasuries. Central banks buying at record rates for 3 consecutive years. The real story is trust, not gold: freezing of Russian reserves post-Ukraine sent unmistakable message — assets inside the system can become inaccessible. Gold has no issuer, no sanctions committee, no counterparty.
items.angle: ECB report moving from niche macro circles to mainstream television is the sentiment shift that accelerates further central bank buying. Reserve diversification thesis is now textbook, not contrarian.
items.tweetA: Gold just officially overtook US Treasuries as the world's largest reserve asset. The ECB confirmed it. But this is not new — it has been building for years. The real turning point was when Western governments froze Russian reserves. Every central bank in the world heard the same message: assets inside the system can be seized. Gold has no issuer. No sanctions committee. That is the trade.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to one line: 'Central banks are not abandoning the dollar. They are building a second layer of protection around it.' Gold is not replacing the dollar. It is replacing the portion of trust that used to be invested in dollar assets unconditionally. That is a much more durable bid.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Gold's recent weakness was a liquidity event masked as geopolitical failure — weak hands exiting, strong hands accumulating
items.idea: When oil prices surged post-Hormuz, energy-importing nations (notably Turkey) mobilized gold reserves for liquidity. This created oil and gold moving in opposite directions despite the same catalyst. 'Markets often confuse price action with ownership. They are not the same thing.'
items.angle: If correct, the gold selloff is a buying opportunity: forced-liquidation seller, not conviction seller. Turkey's mobilization is a known, finite event. The reserve diversification bid is structural.
items.tweetA: Everyone saw gold sell off during the Hormuz crisis and called it a safe-haven failure. Wrong. Energy-importing nations needed liquidity — they sold gold to fund oil import bills. Forced seller, not conviction seller. Weak hands to strong hands. The reserve diversification bid does not go away because Turkey had a funding problem.
items.tweetB: Oil surges because Hormuz closes. Energy-importing central banks sell gold to fund the oil bill. Gold falls despite the geopolitical shock. The crowd says safe-haven is broken. The macro answer: you are watching a liquidity event, not a correlation breakdown. Completely different things with completely different implications.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Treasury market shifting from mandatory buyers to voluntary buyers — demand becomes yield-sensitive
items.idea: Most important long-run implication of gold surpassing Treasuries in reserves: who replaces central banks as Treasury buyers? Portfolio managers who buy because yield is attractive, not reserve managers who buy because they need reserves. 'Treasury demand becomes more sensitive to price. Fiscal deficits matter more.' The system is shifting from mandatory to voluntary demand.
items.angle: Medium-term structural bearish case for Treasuries — not a crisis but a repricing of the demand floor. Every US fiscal deficit discussion needs this context.
items.tweetA: The bigger story buried in the 'gold overtook Treasuries' headline: who replaces central banks as Treasury buyers? Pension funds. Asset managers. Hedge funds. They buy because yield is attractive, not because they need reserves. The Treasury market is moving from mandatory demand to optional demand. That changes the fiscal calculus permanently.
items.tweetB: For 40 years, US Treasury issuance was met by automatic foreign central bank recycling of trade surpluses. That is ending. Not in a crisis — slowly, as gold replaces the trust component. The new buyer is a portfolio manager who can say no. And they will, at the wrong yield. $TLT longs should think about this.

items.title: HFIR flips: Hormuz binary (regime topple or permanent IRGC control), 11M b/d lost daily, diplomacy dead on arrival
items.idea: HFIR wrote the case against Hormuz closure in 2024 and 2025 and has now fully reversed. Three events changed the view: (1) Iran retaliated against GCC; (2) IRGC demonstrated it can forcibly close the Strait mid-April; (3) every day of delay burns more inventory. 11M b/d lost every day. Governments treating it as short-term using SPR are making a strategic error. Only two outcomes: regime topple or permanent IRGC control.
items.angle: A converted skeptic is more credible than a perma-bull. The binary framing means no middle path in the oil market. SPR levels become the countdown clock.
items.tweetA: HFIR was one of the clearest Hormuz skeptics — wrote the case against closure in 2024 and 2025. They just changed their mind completely. The IRGC demonstrated it CAN close the strait in April. Diplomacy is 'dead on arrival.' The only outcomes are regime change or permanent Iranian control of 20% of global oil flow. That is not a geopolitical thesis. That is a supply thesis.
items.tweetB: 11 million barrels a day. Gone. Every single day the Strait of Hormuz stays closed. Governments are releasing SPR thinking this is short-term. HFIR's call: it is not short-term, it is binary. Either the IRGC gets toppled or they control the strait. Every SPR barrel released today is one less for the real crisis. The oil math is brutal.

items.title: Israel-Hezbollah tentative ceasefire; Iran talks at rapid pace; deal: Hormuz open + all mines removed in 30 days
items.idea: Tentative ceasefire in Beirut. Trump says Iran talks at 'rapid pace.' Proposed deal: unrestricted Hormuz + Iran removes all mines in 30 days + 60-day ceasefire. Counter-signal: Iran's President Pezeshkian reportedly submitted resignation, IRGC hardliners ascendant. Wayve Capital: market betting on Iran resolution in 2-3 weeks. This is the longest US Middle East engagement since the Iraq invasion.
items.angle: The 30-day mine removal timeline is the market test. Pezeshkian resignation is the bearish signal hiding behind the ceasefire headline. HFIR binary thesis vs Wayve 2-3 week resolution: two credible views, direct contradiction.
items.tweetA: Beirut ceasefire + Iran talks at 'rapid pace' + proposed deal: Hormuz open, all mines removed in 30 days. Oil eased on the headlines. But here is what is buried: Iran's president reportedly resigned and IRGC hardliners are now ascendant. The people negotiating the deal may not be the people who control the strait. Trade accordingly.
items.tweetB: The Iran deal on the table: 30-day mine removal + 60-day ceasefire. Wayve Capital says market prices resolution in 2-3 weeks. Brent at ~$94.60 already discounting it. But HFIR — who called the closure correctly — says diplomacy with the IRGC is dead on arrival. Two credible views, direct contradiction. The Pezeshkian resignation is the tell.

items.source: Princeton Chen / Daily News Wall + Check On Chain
items.title: BTC breaks below $70K while S&P prints 7,600 all-time high — sharpest crypto-equity divergence of the cycle; $68K is the line
items.idea: Bitcoin fell to $70,261 while S&P crossed 7,600 for the first time. Ascending channel from February ~$60K lows broken twice — failed structure. All four key EMAs stack between $75,110 and $80,846. Required level: weekly close above $75,110. Derivatives OI at record 773,000 BTC with elevated funding and weak spot demand = textbook forced-liquidation setup. If $68K gives way, Feb $60K lows are in play.
items.angle: Broken structure + record OI + elevated funding + crypto-equity divergence = clear setup. Sell rallies below $75K. Watch $68K as the liquidation trigger. Only relative long: $SOL.
items.tweetA: BTC broke below $70,000. S&P just printed an all-time high at 7,600. That gap is not a curiosity — it is the most important divergence in markets right now. Ascending channel broken TWICE. 773K BTC in OI at record levels. Funding still elevated. If $68K goes, $60K is the target. No knife catching.
items.tweetB: Record derivatives OI at 773,000 BTC plus elevated funding rates plus weak spot demand plus broken trendline equals the exact setup for a liquidation cascade. Bulls need a weekly close above $75,110. Until then every rally is distribution. The one crypto that works: $SOL — zero ETF outflow days in all of May, $115M inflows while BTC and ETH bleed.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Check On Chain specializes in on-chain charts)

items.title: SOL: zero ETF outflow days in May, $115M inflows while BTC/ETH bleed — institutional rotation within crypto confirmed
items.idea: SOL ETFs: zero outflow days in May, $115M net inflows. BTC and ETH ETFs experiencing outflows. Strategy sold 32 BTC to fund STRC dividends — small, but raises the question of what happens if they sell more. Strategy holds 818,302 BTC at $75,700 cost basis vs $70,261 current price.
items.angle: SOL as relative crypto long is the cleanest institutional read. Money rotating within crypto, not leaving. MSTR 32-BTC sale is a canary in the leverage coal mine.
items.tweetA: Buried in the BTC sell-off: $SOL ETFs had zero outflow days in all of May. $115M of inflows while BTC and ETH bled. Institutions are not leaving crypto. They are rotating within it. SOL is the relative long. ETH near $1,950 is the relative short. The divergence within crypto is as sharp as the divergence between crypto and equities.
items.tweetB: MicroStrategy sold 32 BTC to fund dividends. Small number — but the market immediately asked: what if they sell more? Strategy holds 818,302 BTC at a $75,700 cost basis. BTC is at $70,261. The 'Strategy as forced buyer' narrative has a flip side. Watch the cost basis spread.

items.title: Marvell +60% in one week: Jensen's $2B investment decoded — SRAM IP, DDR4 CXL, NVLink Fusion leverage, and AMD chess
items.idea: Marvell up 60% in one week. Jensen Huang's $2B investment analyzed with 5 hypotheses: force Amazon off Celestial ASIC, block AMD from custom switches, weaken Broadcom, access Celestial IP as ring modulator backup, access Marvell's dense SRAM IP. Author has no single conviction but notes Jensen's move vastly outperformed his own stock. Author now says AMD CPU story is more interesting than Marvell.
items.angle: Nvidia playing 4D chess across optical interconnects, memory, switching, and ASICs simultaneously. After +60%, relative value has shifted to $AMD per the author.
items.tweetA: Marvell is up 60% in one week. Jensen put $2B in. Why? Five hypotheses: force Amazon off Celestial, block AMD from switches, weaken Broadcom, get ring modulator IP backup, or acquire Marvell's dense SRAM IP. Or all of them. This is what it looks like when the world's most valuable company makes a $2B chess move.
items.tweetB: After Marvell's 60% week, the Irrational Analysis author says: AMD CPU story is more interesting now. The relative value has shifted. $MRVL just priced in a lot of good news. $AMD CPU plus custom silicon story has not. When the most sophisticated semi analyst rotates his watchlist, worth paying attention.

items.title: Marvell Structera CXL: repurposed DDR4 from decommissioned servers at ~85% margins — nobody has noticed
items.idea: Marvell's Structera CXL product allows hyperscalers to repurpose old DDR4 from decommissioned servers as memory-pool expansion. Author rates it as probably Marvell's highest gross margin product at ~85% potential margins. 'Went from worthless garbage to extremely valuable and it seems nobody has noticed.' Competing play: Astera Labs ($ALAB), but Marvell interface IP rated better.
items.angle: Hidden asset not in sell-side models. New revenue from legacy infrastructure that only becomes valuable during a DRAM shortage. Monitor ALAB vs MRVL CXL revenue in upcoming earnings.
items.tweetA: Everyone is talking about Marvell's AI networking chips. Nobody is talking about Structera. Marvell's CXL chip lets hyperscalers repurpose old DDR4 from decommissioned servers instead of fire-selling them on eBay. DRAM prices have gone exponential. Old fully-depreciated DDR4 is suddenly valuable. Potential ~85% gross margins. Zero revenue six months ago. Nobody has noticed.
items.tweetB: The memory shortage is creating a second-order opportunity: any technology that extends the life of existing DRAM stacks gets repriced. Marvell Structera is that technology. $ALAB is the competing CXL play but Irrational Analysis rates Marvell better. CXL goes from dead technology to critical infrastructure when DRAM prices triple.

items.title: Marvell dense SRAM runs at 500mV vs 630mV industry floor — gap so wide it is probably better than Nvidia and AMD internal SRAM
items.idea: Marvell's dense SRAM IP operates at 500mV (pushing to 450mV) vs industry floor of ~630mV. Author says gap is so wide it is 'probably better than most if not all internal SRAM IP from Nvidia, AMD, Broadcom, etc.' IP appears licensable. Critical for any chip design needing dense, power-efficient SRAM — AI accelerators, custom ASICs, CXL controllers.
items.angle: If licensable, becomes rational explanation for Jensen's $2B investment beyond networking and ASICs. Lower operating voltage = dramatically lower power per bit for HPC/AI accelerators.
items.tweetA: Buried in the Marvell note: their dense SRAM IP runs at 500mV. Industry standard floor is ~630mV. In chip design, 130mV is a large efficiency and density advantage. The author says it is probably better than Nvidia's and AMD's internal SRAM. If true, Jensen's $2B investment has a clearer logic than anyone is discussing.
items.tweetB: The most technically interesting part of the Marvell +60% story is not the network chips or CXL. It is the SRAM IP. Operating at 500mV when the floor is 630mV means dramatically lower power per bit. For HPC and AI accelerators that are memory-bandwidth constrained, this matters more than most realize. And it may be licensable.

items.title: April PCE 3.8% YoY — highest since 2023 — savings rate dropping; Warsh Day 11; June FOMC is Test #1
items.idea: April PCE at 3.8% YoY, highest since 2023, savings rate dropping. 10Y yield at 4.44%, 30Y near 5%. Markets pricing zero cuts, down from 1-2 expected at start of 2026. Warsh Day 11 as Fed Chair. June 16-17 FOMC is Test #1. Robin Brooks: 'As soon as the war ends, yields will fall.' June historically worst month for stocks in midterm election years.
items.angle: Asymmetry: Hormuz deal = yields fall sharply + risk-on. No deal = PCE 3.8% + no cuts + stagflation premium. The ceasefire IS the implied rate cut.
items.tweetA: April PCE: 3.8% YoY. Highest since 2023. Savings rate falling. June FOMC in 13 days. Markets pricing zero cuts when they entered 2026 expecting two. And everyone is waiting on an Iran deal that, if it happens, instantly reclassifies this inflation print as war-driven and transitory. The ceasefire is the implied rate cut.
items.tweetB: Robin Brooks at Brookings: 'As soon as the war ends, yields will fall.' That is the simplest macro trade in the room. Long duration on a credible Hormuz deal. The risk: HFIR's version where the IRGC has no incentive to deal. One thesis leads to lower yields. The other leads to stagflation. Both are coherent.

items.title: SpaceX S-1 filed: $1.5T+ valuation, $75B IPO = 3x largest US IPO ever; June 11-12 pricing; Tesla merger talks reported
items.idea: SpaceX S-1 filed at $1.5T+ valuation. $75B IPO would be 3x Alibaba's 2014 record. June 11-12 pricing target. CNBC-reported merger talks between Tesla and SpaceX. Combined entity would be world's 5th-largest corporate Bitcoin treasury at $3.3B.
items.angle: Tesla-SpaceX merger would be largest corporate restructuring in a decade: dominant EVs + dominant space infrastructure + Starlink + $3.3B BTC. The $75B IPO alone resets all size comparisons.
items.tweetA: SpaceX S-1 filed. $1.5T valuation. $75B IPO. 3x the largest US IPO ever. Priced June 11-12. Meanwhile CNBC reports merger talks between Tesla and SpaceX. One entity controlling dominant EVs, dominant space infrastructure, Starlink, and a $3.3B Bitcoin treasury. That is not a company. That is a sovereign.
items.tweetB: The $75B SpaceX IPO would be 3x the largest US IPO ever done. Alibaba's 2014 record: $25B. SpaceX at $1.5T is larger than JPMorgan, larger than Exxon. And Musk apparently wants to merge it with Tesla. The era of the supercorp is not coming — it is here.

items.title: Samsung 45,000-worker strike Day 8 of 18 — HBM/DRAM chokepoint at peak NVDA demand with HBM sold out through 2026
items.idea: Samsung's 45,000-worker strike is in Day 8 of a planned 18-day action. HBM is already sold out through 2026. Samsung is one of three HBM producers. 10 days of strike remain. Micron added a McDonald's of market cap in one week. Direct beneficiaries: SK Hynix and Micron.
items.angle: Days 8-18 are the window to watch. $MU and SK Hynix are direct beneficiaries of Samsung production loss. The memory shortage has a calendar and a countdown.
items.tweetA: Samsung 45,000-worker strike, Day 8 of 18. This is happening as NVDA HBM demand is at its absolute peak and HBM is sold out through 2026. Three companies make HBM at scale: Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron. When one has a 45K-worker strike, the other two's pricing power just went up. $MU is the direct beneficiary.
items.tweetB: The most underreported semiconductor supply story: Samsung's 45K-worker strike entering Day 8. 10 days left. DRAM prices already going exponential. Micron added a McDonald's of market cap in one week. Now the largest HBM producer has a three-week production disruption. The DRAM shortage just found a fresh catalyst.

items.title: Cisco AI orders raised to $9B from $5B; HPE Q2 beat and raised FY guide — capex cycle extending into H2 2026
items.idea: Cisco AI orders pipeline raised to $9B from $5B — 80% increase in one quarter. HPE beat Q2 and raised full-year guidance on AI server and networking demand. NVDA-Anthropic partnership 'scaling very, very quickly' per Jensen. Two independent enterprise infrastructure data points confirming capex cycle extension.
items.angle: Cisco at $9B AI pipeline is the enterprise networking leg — recurring and sticky. When boring plumbing companies beat and raise, the foundation is solid.
items.tweetA: Cisco just raised its AI orders pipeline from $5B to $9B. HPE beat Q2 and raised full-year guidance on AI networking demand. Two separate enterprise infrastructure companies, same signal: the capex cycle is not slowing. Everyone watches NVDA. Meanwhile the picks-and-shovels quietly accelerate. $CSCO $HPE
items.tweetB: The tell that the AI buildout is structural: enterprise networking companies are RAISING guidance, not missing. $CSCO AI pipeline: $5B to $9B in one quarter. $HPE: raised FY guidance. When the boring plumbing companies beat, the foundation is solid. This is not a bubble signal. It is a cycle confirmation.

items.title: Citadel/Millennium/Two Sigma rogue signal review: ~80% of PnL disappears after factor neutralization — most alpha is hidden beta
items.idea: A signal with 2.5 Sharpe and no economic rationale: 'profit you cannot account for is profit you cannot size, hedge, or trust through a regime change.' Standard review: Barra/Axioma regression, strip momentum, value, size, low-vol, short-vol. ~80% of PnL typically disappears. February 5, 2018: VIX more than doubled, XIV lost ~96% overnight, 'strategies marketed as uncorrelated took permanent losses' because the test period never contained the defining event. Drawdown stops: 5-7.5%.
items.angle: Evergreen framework for any strategy that just works for mysterious reasons. In macro, the equivalent is any uncorrelated regime trade — the question is always what hidden short-vol exposure is embedded.
items.tweetA: The most important question in quant finance: is this alpha, or is this a factor I have not named yet? Citadel and Millennium's answer: run the Barra regression. Strip momentum, value, size, low-vol, short-vol. If 80% of PnL disappears, you did not find edge — you found leverage on a crowded premium. Feb 5, 2018: the market finally named what XIV was selling.
items.tweetB: Every strategy that 'just works' for no explainable reason is either genuine alpha or a well-disguised short-volatility book. The XIV blowup — 96% loss overnight — happened to strategies that had looked clean for years because the test period never contained the event that defined them. Unexplained alpha terrifies smart PMs more than it excites them.

items.title: Trump drone-industrial policy: UMAC/RCAT/AVEX/ONDS rallied 23-57% last week — domestic drone manufacturing gets policy tailwinds
items.idea: Trump's drone-industrial policy drove 23-57% rallies in domestic manufacturers last week: UMAC, RCAT, AVEX, ONDS. Connects to: Ukraine lessons on drone effectiveness, DJI ban pushing buyers toward US alternatives, Hormuz conflict intensifying military procurement. US military saw what drones do to ships and tanks and started writing checks.
items.angle: Early innings of domestic drone manufacturing as a policy-driven capex cycle. Small caps with violent moves. Conflict context means procurement is being executed now, not planned.
items.tweetA: Trump drone policy drove 23-57% rallies in UMAC/RCAT/AVEX/ONDS last week. This is the early innings of a domestic drone manufacturing industrial policy — the US military watched Ukraine and started writing checks. These are small caps. The moves are violent. The trend is real and getting a conflict catalyst.
items.tweetB: The Hormuz conflict is accelerating US military modernization in one specific direction: autonomous systems. The DJI ban was the setup. Ukraine was the proof of concept. The Iran conflict is the forcing function. Domestic drone manufacturers are getting policy tailwinds and demand pull simultaneously. $RCAT $UMAC

items.title: SPX grinding into resistance at 7,640/7,610; hedges opened; TMAD model portfolio up 5.27% since May 22
items.idea: SPX grinding toward resistance at 7,640 and 7,610. Key support at 7,588; break opens pullback to 7,519. TMAD opened hedges. Model portfolio up 5.27% since May 26 open. New additions: $MEI and $BWA, with $CSCO also strong. Tuesday's fade from 7,600 ATH described as 'first crack in nine-week melt-up.'
items.angle: Concrete SPX levels for this week. Hedge opening consistent with broader picture: BTC breaking, crypto-equity divergence, index at resistance. $MEI and $BWA breakouts suggest improving breadth beneath AI megacap surface.
items.tweetA: SPX hitting resistance at 7,640-7,610. Support at 7,588; break there means pullback to 7,519. TMAD opened hedges today. The technical picture behind the headlines: the index is at records but knocking on the ceiling, not crashing through it. Thin breadth, big resistance, new hedges. Not a top signal — a coiled spring.
items.tweetB: $MEI and $BWA breaking out in the TMAD scanner today. Methode Electronics and BorgWarner — mid-cycle industrials showing relative strength in a market dominated by AI megacaps. When the boring industrial names start working it tells you something about breadth improving beneath the surface. That is a different kind of health signal.
